Apple___Apple_scab.name: Apple Scab
Apple___Apple_scab.symptoms: Olive-green to black velvety spots on leaves; yellowing and premature leaf drop. Fruit develops brown, corky, and cracked lesions.
Apple___Apple_scab.causes: Fungus (Venturia inaequalis). Overwinters in fallen leaves; spread by wind and rain in cool, wet spring weather.
Apple___Apple_scab.management: Rake and destroy fallen leaves; prune for airflow; plant resistant varieties like 'Liberty'; apply fungicides starting at bud break.

Apple___Black_rot.name: Apple Black Rot
Apple___Black_rot.symptoms: 'Frog-eye' leaf spots (purple margins with tan centers). Fruit shows sunken brown lesions with concentric rings, eventually mummifying. Sunken cankers on branches.
Apple___Black_rot.causes: Fungus (Botryosphaeria obtusa). Thrives in warm, humid conditions; overwinters in mummified fruit and bark cankers.
Apple___Black_rot.management: Remove and destroy mummified fruit and cankered branches; prune dead wood; manage fire blight and insect wounds; use general-purpose fungicides.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.name: Cedar Apple Rust
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.symptoms: Bright yellow-orange spots on upper leaf surfaces; hair-like tubes (aecia) on leaf undersides. Fruit may have raised yellow lesions. Brown galls on alternate host (Juniper/Cedar).
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.causes: Fungus (Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae). Requires two hosts (Apple and Cedar) to complete its life cycle.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.management: Remove nearby cedar trees or galls from them; plant resistant apple varieties like 'Delicious'; apply preventative fungicides from pink bud through petal fall.

Bottle_Gourd___Alternaria_Leaf_Blight.name: Alternaria Leaf Blight
Bottle_Gourd___Alternaria_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Small light-brown spots with concentric 'target' rings on older leaves. Severe cases lead to leaf drying and premature defoliation.
Bottle_Gourd___Alternaria_Leaf_Blight.causes: Fungus (Alternaria cucumerina). Favored by warm, humid weather; spread by wind and rain.
Bottle_Gourd___Alternaria_Leaf_Blight.management: Use disease-free seeds; practice 2-year crop rotation; remove crop debris; apply fungicides like Mancozeb.

Bottle_Gourd___Anthracnose.name: Anthracnose
Bottle_Gourd___Anthracnose.symptoms: Water-soaked lesions turning into sunken dark brown or black spots on leaves and fruit. Pinkish spore masses may ooze in humid weather.
Bottle_Gourd___Anthracnose.causes: Fungus (Colletotrichum lagenarium). Thrives in high humidity and moderate temperatures.
Bottle_Gourd___Anthracnose.management: Hot water seed treatment; deep plowing to bury debris; avoid overhead irrigation; apply Carbendazim or Mancozeb.

Bottle_Gourd___Downy_Mildew.name: Downy Mildew
Bottle_Gourd___Downy_Mildew.symptoms: Angular yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces; grayish-purple 'downy' growth on leaf undersides. Leaves shrivel and die quickly.
Bottle_Gourd___Downy_Mildew.causes: Fungus-like organism (Pseudoperonospora cubensis). Highly dependent on leaf wetness and high humidity.
Bottle_Gourd___Downy_Mildew.management: Increase plant spacing for airflow; use resistant varieties; apply fungicides like Metalaxyl or Cymoxanil mixed with Mancozeb.

Bottle_Gourd___Early_Alternaria_Leaf_Bli.name: Early Alternaria Leaf Blight
Bottle_Gourd___Early_Alternaria_Leaf_Bli.symptoms: Initial stage of Alternaria blight showing small, circular light-brown spots on lower leaves before concentric rings develop.
Bottle_Gourd___Early_Alternaria_Leaf_Bli.causes: Fungus (Alternaria cucumerina). Favored by warm, humid conditions.
Bottle_Gourd___Early_Alternaria_Leaf_Bli.management: Sanitation; crop rotation; seed treatment with Trichoderma viride; foliar sprays of Neem oil.

Bottle_Gourd___Fungal_Damage_Leaf.name: Fungal Damage Leaf
Bottle_Gourd___Fungal_Damage_Leaf.symptoms: General fungal symptoms including necrotic spots with yellow halos, white powdery patches, or sudden wilting.
Bottle_Gourd___Fungal_Damage_Leaf.causes: Various fungal pathogens favored by leaf wetness and poor field sanitation.
Bottle_Gourd___Fungal_Damage_Leaf.management: Reduce leaf wetness via drip irrigation; maintain field sanitation; apply broad-spectrum fungicides if specific cause is unknown.

Bottle_Gourd___Hole_Angular_Leaf_Spot.name: Angular Leaf Spot
Bottle_Gourd___Hole_Angular_Leaf_Spot.symptoms: Water-soaked angular spots delimited by veins; spots dry and fall out, creating a 'shot-hole' effect. White bacterial crust may appear.
Bottle_Gourd___Hole_Angular_Leaf_Spot.causes: Bacterium (Pseudomonas syringae pv. lachrymans). Seed-borne; spread by splashing water.
Bottle_Gourd___Hole_Angular_Leaf_Spot.management: Use certified pathogen-free seeds; avoid working in wet fields; spray copper-based fungicides or Streptomycin.

Bottle_Gourd___Mosaic_Virus.name: Bottle Gourd Mosaic Virus
Bottle_Gourd___Mosaic_Virus.symptoms: Light and dark green mosaic patterns on leaves; leaf curling, puckering, and dark green blistering. Stunted growth and deformed fruit.
Bottle_Gourd___Mosaic_Virus.causes: Various viruses (e.g., CMV) transmitted primarily by aphids and whiteflies.
Bottle_Gourd___Mosaic_Virus.management: Uproot and burn infected plants; control aphid vectors using Neem oil or Imidacloprid; keep field free of weeds.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.name: Cherry Powdery Mildew
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White, patches of felt-like fungal growth on leaves; leaves may pucker, curl upward, and become brittle. Fruit may show white powdery bloom.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.causes: Fungus (Podosphaera clandestina). Overwinters in buds; favored by warm temperatures and high humidity.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.management: Prune for air circulation; remove water sprouts; apply sulfur or strobilurin fungicides starting at bloom.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.name: Gray Leaf Spot (Cercospora Leaf Spot)
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.symptoms: Long, narrow, rectangular tan lesions strictly delimited by veins; lesions may show a silvery-gray cast in high humidity.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.causes: Fungus (Cercospora zeae-maydis). Overwinters in corn residue; favored by warm, very humid conditions.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.management: Practice crop rotation and tillage; plant resistant hybrids; apply foliar fungicides if disease pressure is high at tasseling.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.name: Common Rust
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.symptoms: Small, cinnamon-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces; pustules rupture to release powdery spores and turn brownish-black.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.causes: Fungus (Puccinia sorghi). Spores blown from southern regions; favored by cool, moist weather.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.management: Plant resistant hybrids; early planting to avoid high inoculum levels; fungicides for specialty corn if necessary.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.name: Northern Leaf Blight
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Large, elliptical, 'cigar-shaped' tan or grayish lesions up to 6 inches long; dark green/black fuzzy spore masses in center during humid weather.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.causes: Fungus (Exserohilum turcicum). Overwinters in corn residue; favored by moderate temperatures and extended leaf wetness.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.management: Use resistant hybrids (race-specific or partial); crop rotation and tillage; fungicides applied before or at tasseling.

Grape___Black_rot.name: Grape Black Rot
Grape___Black_rot.symptoms: Small circular tan spots with dark brown borders on leaves. Berries rot rapidly and shrivel into hard, black 'mummies'.
Grape___Black_rot.causes: Fungus (Guignardia bidwellii). Overwinters in mummies and cane lesions; favored by warm, wet weather.
Grape___Black_rot.management: Sanitation: remove mummified fruit; prune for air circulation; apply protective fungicides from early shoot growth through 4 weeks after bloom.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).name: Esca (Black Measles)
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).symptoms: 'Tiger-stripe' foliar patterns (interveinal yellow/red patches). Berries develop dark spots with brown-purple rings ('measles').
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).causes: Complex of fungi (e.g., Phaeomoniella chlamydospora) entering through pruning wounds.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).management: Avoid pruning in wet weather; use double pruning or wound protectants; remove and burn infected wood; reduce vine stress.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.name: Grape Leaf Blight
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.symptoms: Large, irregular, angular spots, initially dull red/brown on upper surface, turning black and brittle. Premature leaf drop.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.causes: Fungus (Pseudocercospora vitis). Minor disease appearing late in the season.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.management: Improve air circulation; destroy crop residues; standard fungicide programs for other grape diseases usually control it.

Mango___Anthracnose.name: Mango Anthracnose
Mango___Anthracnose.symptoms: Sunken, dark brown to black spots on leaves, flowers, and fruits. Causes blossom blight and fruit rot in storage.
Mango___Anthracnose.causes: Fungus (Colletotrichum gloeosporioides). Thrives in high humidity and rainy conditions.
Mango___Anthracnose.management: Prune infected twigs; apply copper-based fungicides before flowering; post-harvest hot water treatment for fruit.

Mango___Bacterial_Canker.name: Mango Bacterial Canker
Mango___Bacterial_Canker.symptoms: Angular, water-soaked black raised spots on leaves. Fruits develop star-shaped cracks and ooze sticky bacterial fluid.
Mango___Bacterial_Canker.management: Use disease-free planting material; prune and burn infected parts; apply Streptomycin or Copper Oxychloride.

Mango___Die_Back.name: Mango Die Back
Mango___Die_Back.symptoms: Twigs and branches dry from tip downwards; leaves turn brown, curl, and remain attached.
Mango___Die_Back.causes: Fungus (Lasiodiplodia theobromae). Triggered by plant stress like drought or nutrient deficiency.
Mango___Die_Back.management: Prune branches 3 inches below infection; apply copper fungicides or Carbendazim to cut ends.

Mango___Gall_Midge.name: Mango Gall Midge
Mango___Gall_Midge.symptoms: Small, raised, wart-like galls on young leaves. Infested leaves become deformed, curl, and drop prematurely.
Mango___Gall_Midge.causes: Tiny flies (Procontarinia spp.) whose larvae feed inside leaf tissue, forming galls.
Mango___Gall_Midge.management: Prune and destroy infested shoots; spray systemic insecticides like Imidacloprid during new flushes.

Mango___Powdery_Mildew.name: Mango Powdery Mildew
Mango___Powdery_Mildew.symptoms: White, powdery fungal growth on leaves, flower stalks, and young fruits, causing them to shed prematurely.
Mango___Powdery_Mildew.causes: Fungus (Oidium mangiferae). Favored by cool nights and high humidity during flowering.
Mango___Powdery_Mildew.management: Apply sulfur-based or systemic fungicides like Hexaconazole at the start of flowering.

Mango___Sooty_Mould.name: Mango Sooty Mould
Mango___Sooty_Mould.symptoms: Black, velvety film covering leaves and fruits, blocking sunlight and reducing photosynthesis.
Mango___Sooty_Mould.causes: Fungi (Capnodium spp.) growing on honeydew secreted by sap-sucking insects.
Mango___Sooty_Mould.management: Control insects (aphids, hoppers) using neem oil or insecticides; wash off mould with starch solution or soap.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).name: Huanglongbing (Citrus Greening)
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).symptoms: Asymmetrical yellow mottling ('blotchy mottle') on leaves. Fruits are small, lopsided, bitter, and stay green at the bottom.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).causes: Bacterium (Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus), spread by the Asian Citrus Psyllid.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).management: No cure; use pathogen-free nursery stock; remove infected trees immediately; control the psyllid vector with insecticides.

Papaya___Bacterial_Blight.name: Papaya Bacterial Blight
Papaya___Bacterial_Blight.symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaf stalks; leaves turn yellow and collapse. Growing tip may rot, leading to plant death.
Papaya___Bacterial_Blight.management: Remove and burn infected plants; avoid overhead irrigation to reduce moisture on the crown.

Papaya___Carica_Insect_Hole.name: Insect Damage (Holes)
Papaya___Carica_Insect_Hole.symptoms: Small holes, pimples, or galls on leaves; distorted or curled young leaves.
Papaya___Carica_Insect_Hole.causes: Gall midges, mites, or thrips feeding on leaf tissue.
Papaya___Carica_Insect_Hole.management: Prune infested parts; use miticides or neem-based sprays; systemic insecticides for gall midges.

Papaya___Curled_Yellow_Spot.name: Yellow Spot / Leaf Curl
Papaya___Curled_Yellow_Spot.symptoms: White powdery patches on leaf undersides; upper surfaces show yellow spots. Leaves may curl and drop.
Papaya___Curled_Yellow_Spot.causes: Fungus (Oidium caricae) or viral pathogens spread by insects.
Papaya___Curled_Yellow_Spot.management: Dust with sulfur; spray fungicides like Calixin; control insect vectors to prevent viral spread.

Papaya___Pathogen_Symptoms.name: General Papaya Pathogen Symptoms
Papaya___Pathogen_Symptoms.symptoms: Water-soaked spots, yellowing, or necrotic patches on leaves and fruit, often leading to rot.
Papaya___Pathogen_Symptoms.causes: Various bacterial or fungal pathogens favored by high humidity and rain.
Papaya___Pathogen_Symptoms.management: Improve drainage; practice sanitation; avoid wounding plants; apply protective copper sprays.

Papaya___Yellow_Necrotic_Spots_Holes.name: Yellow Necrotic Spots and Holes
Papaya___Yellow_Necrotic_Spots_Holes.symptoms: Yellow spots that turn necrotic (brown/dead) and eventually fall out, leaving holes in the leaves.
Papaya___Yellow_Necrotic_Spots_Holes.causes: Fungal infections like Alternaria or Bacterial spot; sometimes mechanical or insect damage followed by infection.
Papaya___Yellow_Necrotic_Spots_Holes.management: Sanitation; remove infected leaves; apply fungicides or bactericides based on specific diagnosis.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.name: Peach Bacterial Spot
Peach___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, angular, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn purple/black and fall out ('shot-hole'). Fruits develop deep pits or cracks.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.management: Plant resistant varieties; apply copper sprays during dormant season; use Oxytetracycline during growing season.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.name: Pepper Bacterial Spot
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, irregular water-soaked spots on leaves with yellow halos. Fruit spots are brown, raised, and scabby.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.causes: Bacterium (Xanthomonas vesicatoria). Introduced via seeds; spread by splashing water.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.management: Use certified disease-free seeds; practice crop rotation; use drip irrigation; apply copper-based bactericides.

Potato___Early_blight.name: Potato Early Blight
Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown, circular spots with concentric 'target' rings on older leaves. Tubers show dark, sunken, corky rot.
Potato___Early_blight.causes: Fungus (Alternaria solani). Favored by alternating wet and dry periods.
Potato___Early_blight.management: Use resistant varieties; rotate crops; maintain plant vigor; apply fungicides like chlorothalonil.

Potato___Late_blight.name: Potato Late Blight
Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large, irregular 'greasy' dark-brown spots on leaves. White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides in humid weather. Tubers show reddish-brown rot.
Potato___Late_blight.causes: Water mold (Phytophthora infestans). Favored by cool, wet, and humid conditions.
Potato___Late_blight.management: Plant certified disease-free seed; destroy volunteer plants; use preventative copper or mancozeb fungicides.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.name: Squash Powdery Mildew
Squash___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: Circular white powdery spots on upper leaf surfaces, stems, and petioles. Leaves eventually yellow, brown, and shrivel.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.causes: Fungus (Podosphaera xanthii). Thrives in warm, dry climates with high relative humidity around the plant.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.management: Plant resistant varieties; improve airflow and sun exposure; use neem oil, sulfur, or milk-water sprays.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.name: Strawberry Leaf Scorch
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.symptoms: Irregular purplish spots on upper leaf surfaces. Leaves may turn red/purple, dry up, and curl at edges ('scorched' look).
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.causes: Fungus (Diplocarpon earliana). Spreads via splashing water; severe in moderate temperatures and wet conditions.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.management: Remove and destroy infected debris; use drip irrigation; improve air circulation; replant every few years.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.name: Tomato Bacterial Spot
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked dark spots on leaves, often with a yellow halo. Fruit shows small black specks that become brown, scabby, raised spots.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.causes: Bacterium (Xanthomonas spp.). Introduced via seeds or transplants; spread by rain or overhead irrigation.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.management: Use pathogen-free seeds; avoid overhead irrigation; apply copper-based bactericides mixed with mancozeb.

Tomato___Early_blight.name: Tomato Early Blight
Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown, circular spots with concentric 'target' rings on older leaves. Can cause stem lesions and fruit rot.
Tomato___Early_blight.causes: Fungus (Alternaria solani). Favored by warm, wet conditions; often affects stressed plants.
Tomato___Early_blight.management: Crop rotation; mulch to prevent soil splashing; remove lower infected leaves; apply chlorothalonil.

Tomato___Late_blight.name: Tomato Late Blight
Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large, irregular water-soaked spots on leaves. White fuzzy growth on undersides. Fruits develop large, firm, brown, greasy-looking spots.
Tomato___Late_blight.causes: Water mold (Phytophthora infestans). Extremely aggressive in cool, wet weather.
Tomato___Late_blight.management: Plant resistant varieties; avoid overhead watering; rogue infected plants immediately; apply copper fungicides.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.name: Tomato Leaf Mold
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces; velvety olive-brown fungal growth on the underside. Leaves shrivel and drop.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.causes: Fungus (Passalora fulva). Thrives in very high humidity (above 85%), common in greenhouses.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.management: Improve ventilation to lower humidity; use drip irrigation; plant resistant cultivars.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.name: Septoria Leaf Spot
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Small circular spots with dark brown margins and tan/grey centers containing tiny black specks. Starts on lower leaves.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.causes: Fungus (Septoria lycopersici). Spreads via splashing water from rain or irrigation.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.management: Crop rotation; remove infected debris; mulch to prevent soil splash; apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.name: Two-spotted Spider Mite
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.symptoms: Tiny white/yellow stippling on upper leaf surfaces; leaves may turn bronze. Fine silky webbing on undersides in heavy infestations.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.causes: Tiny arachnids (Tetranychus urticae). Thrive in hot, dry, dusty conditions.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.management: Keep plants well-watered; hose mites off with water; use insecticidal soaps, neem oil, or predatory insects.

Tomato___Target_Spot.name: Tomato Target Spot
Tomato___Target_Spot.symptoms: Necrotic leaf spots with light centers and dark margins, showing concentric rings. Fruit has sunken craters with concentric circles.
Tomato___Target_Spot.causes: Fungus (Corynespora cassiicola). Favored by high humidity and long periods of leaf wetness.
Tomato___Target_Spot.management: Improve airflow; avoid overhead irrigation; rotate crops away from solanaceous hosts for 3 years; use fungicides.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.name: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Severe stunting; new leaves are small, yellow between veins, and curl upward like a cup. Reduced fruit production.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.causes: Virus transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.management: Control whiteflies with insecticides or sticky traps; use resistant varieties; immediately rogue and destroy infected plants.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.name: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV)
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mottling of leaves with light and dark green patches; leaf curling, twisting, or stunting. Fruit may show internal browning.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.causes: Extremely stable virus spread by contaminated tools, hands, clothing, and seeds.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.management: Use certified seeds and resistant varieties; strict sanitation (wash hands, disinfect tools); rogue infected plants.

Zucchini___Angular_Leaf_Spot.name: Angular Leaf Spot
Zucchini___Angular_Leaf_Spot.symptoms: Water-soaked gray-green angular spots limited by veins; spots dry and fall out leaving 'shot-holes'. White bacterial ooze on fruit.
Zucchini___Angular_Leaf_Spot.causes: Bacterium (Pseudomonas syringae pv. lachrymans). Favored by warm, wet, and humid conditions.
Zucchini___Angular_Leaf_Spot.management: Certified disease-free seeds; 2-3 year crop rotation; avoid overhead irrigation; apply copper-based bactericides.

Zucchini___Anthracnose.name: Anthracnose
Zucchini___Anthracnose.symptoms: Yellowish water-soaked spots on leaves turning brown/black. Sunken lesions on fruit with pinkish spore masses in humid weather.
Zucchini___Anthracnose.causes: Fungus (Colletotrichum orbiculare). Spread by splashing rain, wind, and contaminated tools.
Zucchini___Anthracnose.management: Plant resistant varieties; destroy infected debris; avoid working when plants are wet; use fungicides like chlorothalonil.

Zucchini___Downy_Mildew.name: Downy Mildew
Zucchini___Downy_Mildew.symptoms: Angular yellow spots on upper surfaces; fuzzy gray-to-purple mold on undersides. Leaves brown and die, but fruit is usually unaffected.
Zucchini___Downy_Mildew.causes: Water mold (Pseudoperonospora cubensis). Requires high humidity and cool-to-moderate temperatures.
Zucchini___Downy_Mildew.management: Improve air circulation; water early in the day; apply preventative copper or mancozeb fungicides.

Zucchini___Dry_Leaf.name: Dry Leaf Symptoms
Zucchini___Dry_Leaf.symptoms: Leaf edges or entire leaves turn brown, brittle, and crispy.
Zucchini___Dry_Leaf.causes: Environmental stress (drought, windburn, extreme heat), old age, or final stages of other diseases.
Zucchini___Dry_Leaf.management: Consistent root-zone moisture; use mulch; prune off old dried lower leaves.

Zucchini___Insect_Damage.name: General Insect Damage
Zucchini___Insect_Damage.symptoms: Holes in leaves (beetles), wilting of vines (borers), or yellow-speckled leaves (bugs).
Zucchini___Insect_Damage.causes: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, aphids, or squash vine borers.
Zucchini___Insect_Damage.management: Hand-pick pests; yellow sticky traps or row covers; inject Bt for borers; use neem oil for aphids.

Zucchini___Iron_Chlorosis_Damage.name: Iron Chlorosis
Zucchini___Iron_Chlorosis_Damage.symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis (yellow tissue between green veins). In severe cases, leaves turn almost white with scorched edges.
Zucchini___Iron_Chlorosis_Damage.causes: Iron deficiency, usually due to high soil pH (alkaline) or poorly drained soil.
Zucchini___Iron_Chlorosis_Damage.management: Test and lower soil pH; apply chelated iron (soil or foliar); improve soil drainage.

Zucchini___Xanthomonas_Leaf_Spot.name: Bacterial Leaf Spot
Zucchini___Xanthomonas_Leaf_Spot.symptoms: Small round water-soaked spots on leaf undersides, yellow on top. Spots turn brown with yellow halos. Sunken beige spots on fruit.
Zucchini___Xanthomonas_Leaf_Spot.causes: Bacterium (Xanthomonas campestris pv. cucurbitae). Introduced via infected seeds.
Zucchini___Xanthomonas_Leaf_Spot.management: Use clean seeds; 2-year crop rotation; avoid overhead watering; copper sprays can help limit spread.

Zucchini___Yellow_Mosaic_Virus.name: Zucchini Yellow Mosaic Virus (ZYMV)
Zucchini___Yellow_Mosaic_Virus.symptoms: Yellow mosaic/mottled patterns; severe leaf distortion, blistering, or 'shoestring' effect. Stunted plants and bumpy fruit.
Zucchini___Yellow_Mosaic_Virus.causes: Virus transmitted primarily by aphids and mechanical contact.
Zucchini___Yellow_Mosaic_Virus.management: Plant resistant varieties; control aphids with neem oil or soap; immediately remove and destroy infected plants.
